# Transcrição: 03-Hand Gestures

- **Fonte:** 03-Hand Gestures

## [00:00 - 03:02]

This is my favorite area of body language. Hand gestures. These things. People are really scared of these things because often they go, I don't know what to do with these. What do I do with them? Do I just, do I holster them? What do I do? And the problem is when you don't know what to do with your hands and you don't understand the foundations to hand gestures, what do, let's pick on the men first. What do most men do when they don't know what to do with their hands? They go, shh, shh, and they put them in their pockets. And then, you know, after a while they get comfortable here and then they start to gesture with their hands inside their pockets and people thinking, what are you doing in your pants? Are you trying to find keys? And then I'm not going to leave the women out of this as well. What do women do when they don't know what to do with their hands? Often they tend to put them on the hips. Or sometimes both men and women, what do you do? You fold your arms. And then sometimes we kind of put our hands behind our back, right? Or sometimes we do the classic one hand in pocket and then gesture with just one hand. Look, this is okay, but it's not effective. Because what are hand gestures for? Hand gestures when it comes to body language, it helps us support the things that we're saying. Notice what I just did there? To me, this was supporting the word support. So I can show you again without that. I can say, hand gestures help support the things that you're saying. Which one was more effective? The one with the hand gestures. And we can't talk about hand gestures without giving credit to a lady named Virginia Satia. Virginia Satia was a family therapist and she came up with the foundations to hand gestures. Virginia called this the satia categories. The satia categories of which there are five core foundations when it comes to hand gestures. The first one, the first one is called placata. Now placata looks like this. Hands palm face up. This is a really nice opening kind of gesture you can use when you're starting a speech, when you're walking into a room and having a meeting and you're going, hey everyone, thanks for being here. It's very inviting. It's non-threatening. And it's non-threatening because it communicates to your reptilian brain, I have no weapons. Empty hands. While this is a very open and very inviting gesture, it lacks authority. And you notice this when you see parents telling off their kids, for example. When I'm telling Xander off and I'm telling him off for doing something wrong and I say, Xander, stop doing that. Do you notice how this gesture is very closely related to its cousin? I don't know. Notice how it's very closely related to that. So when you say Xander, stop doing that, it lacks authority, doesn't it? This is why the second gesture is so critical. The second gesture is known as levella. And levella looks like this. His palm face down. This has a lot more authority, doesn't it? I mean, I want you to try something and just try something right now, even though you're

sitting at home or even in the office, don't be afraid to try this. Just try this. I want you to say, Xander, stop doing that with placata, first of all. Versus Xander, stop doing that. Notice the difference? Your voice changes. There's more authority in your voice when you use levella. And again, that's proof that our body moves our voice and our voice moves our body. They're both completely interconnected. Now you don't want to get stuck in levella, just like how you don't want to get stuck in placata. Because if you got stuck in placata and this is your default gesture, then again, you may come across as someone who lacks authority. And then you, on the opposite of that, don't want to be stuck in levella because you may come across with too much authority and it's overwhelming sometimes. And people can say that you come across a little bit aggressive. Now I'll give you an example. So blamer when you really want to highlight something. Now this next gesture, the fourth gesture, it's called computer. And computer looks like this. You can be touching your chin. ## [06:04 - 09:06]

or the person that you're listening to that I'm processing what you're saying. I'm computing what you're saying. It's a way for you to visually show that I'm listening, that I care, that I'm engaged. This is a great gesture when we're listening to people virtually, when we're connecting with people online. It's a nice way to show people that, hey, I'm actually paying attention. Otherwise sometimes what happens when we're listening? What happens? Sometimes we get stuck in the default face and we have terrible body language and we're just like. And then people are thinking, wait, does this person even care? And again, when we are listening, we often don't have awareness as to what our face looks like and what our body is doing, especially when we're completely engaged. So you want to have access to this particular gesture because again, it shows people that I'm listening and I'm engaged. And the final gesture, the final one that Virginia talks about is called distractor. And distractor is used as a pattern break. I'll show you what I mean. We use distractor when we notice that we're losing the audience's attention. Let's say for example, when you're talking, you're talking, you notice, oh, I'm kind of losing them here. What you can do is you can use distractor. So I'll give you another example. So I'm talking, talking, losing the audience's attention. Oh, look, I'm sorry. Let's go back to the beginning. I know I'm losing you. Did you notice what I did? It's just a quick auditory clap. So it's an auditory pattern break followed by what I call shooing bees. So just kind of, oh, I'm so sorry. Let's kind of go back to the beginning. I could tell I'm losing you there. That's just a nice little pattern break that snaps them out of their kind of state of lack of focus and brings their focus back. And that's what you're able to do with distractor. Now you rarely use distractor, but when you do, it's extremely effective. Quick note here. You don't want to always be doing this because if you always have to snap people out of their daydreaming, it means you need to improve your presentation or make it more relevant. When I was in the U.S., I was lucky enough to be coached and mentored by Mark Bodin. He's one of the leading experts in body language in the world. And while being coached by him, he taught me about the power sphere. And the power sphere is the area between your belly button and your eyes. So if you keep your hand gestures between your belly button and your eyes kind of up here, this is known as the power sphere. This is where your hand gestures are the most effective. And I mean, you want to avoid gesturing from down here. And I know it seems a bit ridiculous, you know, why would you gesture down here? But believe it or not, some people actually do. They keep most of their gestures down here. So avoid that, keep your hand gestures up and within the power sphere. Now I know this is not going to be a problem because I don't think I've ever come across anybody who gestures up here, that would be kind of crazy. But just keep that in mind, the power sphere. The last thing I'm going to mention about hand gestures here is that when you first learn about the foundational gestures, you're going to feel a little bit weird doing them. You're going to say, oh, I feel like I'm dancing and you feel very awkward and strange about it. You seem a bit clunky.
